videoTitle: Lesson 5.1- What is a Marketing Funnel (TOFU, MOFU, BOFU).mp4
transcript: Welcome to Module 5 and Lesson 5.1. What is a Marketing Funnel? Covering Tofu, Mofu, and Bofu. If you have spent time in marketing, you have heard these terms before. But in this lesson, you are going to understand them at a deeper, more strategic level. Specifically, how they connect to the Go High Level automations you will be building from this module forward. By the end of this lesson, you will know what a marketing funnel is and why every business needs one, the three funnel stages and what your lead needs at each stage, how each stage maps to specific tools inside Go High Level, and why mapping your funnel before building anything makes your entire automation system more effective. Let's get into it. So what exactly is a marketing funnel? A marketing funnel is a visual model that maps the stages a person moves through, from first discovering a business all the way to making a purchase decision and becoming a customer. The word funnel comes from the shape itself. At the top, it's wide. Many people enter. As they move through each stage, fewer and fewer remain, because not everyone is a good fit and not everyone is ready to buy right now. By the bottom, you have a smaller group of people who are highly qualified, engaged, and ready to take action. Here's the key principle that changes how you think about every campaign and automation you build. The funnel shows you where your leads are and what they need next. When you know the stage, you know the message. When you know the message, you know which automation to build. The clarity is the power of thinking in funnels. Not everyone who enters will become a customer, and that is not a failure. It is the funnel working exactly as designed, filtering out the wrong fits and guiding the right people forward. Before we break down the three stages, I want to make sure you understand why the funnel matters specifically for your work as a marketing automation specialist. Every automation you build in Go High Level serves a specific funnel stage. This is not abstract. It is the direct connection between strategy and execution. At the top of the funnel, your Go High Level work involves the landing pages and lead capture forms you built in Module 4, plus the welcome sequences that greet new leads when they first opt in. At the middle of the funnel, your GHL work involves the email nurture sequences you will build in Module 6, the case study delivery workflows, and the consultation booking funnels. At the bottom of the funnel, your GHL work involves the offer pages, The urgency-based follow-up workflows from Lesson 7.6 and the purchase confirmation automations. Without a funnel framework, you might build technically correct automations that fire at the wrong moment. Sending a sales pitch to someone who has never heard of the business. Or sending a welcome email to someone who has been on the list for three months. The funnel gives every workflow a clear purpose and a strategic position in the customer journey. That is why we are learning this before we design anything in Miro. Let's break down each stage, starting with Tofu, top of funnel. Tofu is the awareness stage. The people here are strangers. They have never heard of the business They might have a problem but have not yet found a solution They are browsing exploring and in early discovery mode What do these people need? They need to become aware that solutions exist. They need educational content like blog posts, social media, videos that addresses their problem. They do not need a sales pitch. Showing up with a hard sell at this stage is the fastest way to lose them. What do you build in Go High Level at Tofu? You build the lead magnet landing pages you created in Module 4, specifically using the GHL funnel builder from Lesson 4.5. You build forms that capture name and email in exchange for free value. and you build the welcome email sequences that are triggered the moment someone opts in. The single most important principle at Tofu, you are not selling. You are educating and attracting. Every Tofu touchpoint should make the person feel helped, not pressured. Trust is the only currency that works here. MoFu is the middle of funnel, the consideration stage. These are people who have opted in or shown interest. They know the business exists. Now, they are asking, can I trust these people? Are they the right fit for me? The people here are actively comparing options. They are reading emails, watching content, maybe attending a webinar. They are building a relationship with the brand. But the relationship is still fragile. One poor experience, an irrelevant email, a delayed response, a generic message can lose them. What do mofo leads need? They need proof that the business can actually solve their problem. They need social proof. Testimonials from real people who are in the same situation. They need their specific objections addressed. They need to feel that this brand understands them personally. What do you build in Go High Level at MoFu? You build the email nurture sequences you will learn in Module 6. You build workflows that automatically deliver case studies at the right moment. You build the consultation or webinar booking funnels that create the next opportunity for deeper connection. The principle at MoFu, you are building trust. The lead already knows you exist. Now you need them to believe you are the right choice. Every automation at this stage earns that belief one interaction at a time. Both woo is the bottom of funnel, the decision stage. This is where your warmest, most engaged leads live. They trust the brand. They have consumed multiple pieces of content and they are ready to make a decision. The people here are not asking whether to buy. They are asking whether to buy now. Their question is, is this the right moment and is this the right offer? They need clarity on what they will receive, confidence that the risk is low, and often a reason to act now rather than waiting. What do Bofo prospects need? They need a clear, compelling offer with transparent pricing. They need risk removal, a guarantee. A free trial or a clear refund policy. And they often need urgency or scarcity that gives them a genuine reason to act today. What do you build in Grow High Level at Bofu? You build sales pages and checkout flows using the GHL funnel builder. You build abandoned cart and follow-up sequences covered in Lesson 7.6. You build appointment booking automation with reminder workflows so that even after someone books the system keeps them engaged and confirmed until the appointment happens The principle at Bofu, remove friction. These people want to buy. Your job is to make the path to purchasing clear, easy, and feel like the obvious next step. Let me now show you all three stages together so you can see how they differ and how they connect. At Tofu, you are dealing with cold strangers. Their question is, what is this? Your goal is to attract and capture. The content type is educational like blog posts, SEO content, social media, and lead magnets. In Go High Level, you are using the Funnel Builder from Lesson 4.5 to build lead capture pages. At MoFu, you are dealing with warm leads. Their question is, can I trust this? Your goal is to nurture and educate. The content type is relationship building, email sequences, webinars, case studies. In GoHighLevel, you are using the Email Campaign Builder from Module 6. At Bofu, you are dealing with hot prospects. Their question is, should I buy now? Your goal is to convert and close. The content type is offer-focused. Sales pages, guarantees, time-sensitive offers. In Go High Level, you are using pipelines and workflows from Module 7. Notice how each stage has a different visitor, a different question, a different goal, and different GHL tools. This is exactly why you need to know the funnel stage before you build any automation. The wrong message at the wrong stage wastes your effort and alienates the lead. Here is one of the most important mindset shifts I want you to have about funnels. Drop-off is normal, expected, and healthy. Imagine 1,000 people see your social media content at Tofu. Of those, 100 opt in your email list. Of those 100, roughly 10 become paying customers. That is a 1% overall conversion rate from strangers to customer. And that is actually common across many industries. 3-5% from top to bottom is considered excellent. Why does this not mean failure? Because the funnel is doing its job as a filter. It's naturally removing people who are not a good fit, not ready to buy, or simply not interested in this particular offer. The 10 customers who converted are qualified, motivated, and likely to stick around and buy again. They are worth far more than 1,000 unqualified contacts who cost you time and resources. Your job as a marketing automation specialist is not to convert 100% of people who enter a funnel. It's to optimize each stage individually. If your Tofu opt-in rate improves from 10% to 20%, you go from 100 leads to 200 leads. And those extra 100 leads produces an additional 10 customers without any traffic. Improving each stage by even 10% can double your total conversions. That is the power of understanding and optimizing the funnel stage by stage. Now let me show you exactly how this funnel framework maps to the rest of your learning in this course. Because everything from Module 4 forward serves a specific funnel stage. At Tofu the work you did in Module 4 building landing pages in GoHighLevel is the entrypoint of every funnel The lead magnet landing page you built using the JHL Funnel Builder from Lesson 4 are TOFU tools They capture cold traffic and bring people into your automation system. Module 3's inbound and outbound campaigns are also TOFU work, driving people to those pages. At MoFu, Module 5 Funnel Design in Miro gives you the visual blueprint for your entire nurture strategy. Module 6 Email and SMS campaigns are the content delivery system for MoFu. They send the right messages to keep warm leads engaged in moving forward decisions. At BOFU, Module 7 workflows and automation sequences handle the closing mechanisms. Follow-ups, appointment booking, abandoned cart recovery, pipeline management. And Module 8 analytics tells you how well each stage of your funnel is performing so you can optimize continuously. Here is what is especially important for the rest of Module 5. In Lesson 5.4, you will learn to use Miro to visually map funnels. In Lesson 5.5, you will design a complete funnel in Miro step-by-step, mapping out every Tofu, Mofu, and Bofu touchpoint before a single workflow is built and go high level. That design-before-build approach is exactly what professional marketing automation specialists do. And it starts with the framework you learned today. Let's lock in what you learned in this lesson. A marketing funnel is a model mapping the stages someone moves through from stranger to customer. It shows you where your leads are and what they need next at every stage. TOFU, top of funnel, is the awareness stage. Cold traffic discovers the business and you capture them with lead magnet landing pages and welcome sequences built in Go High Level using the Funnel Builder from Lesson 4.5. MOFU, Middle of Funnel, is the Consideration Stage. Warm leads are nurtured through email sequences, case studies, and trust-building content that will be built in Module 6. BOFU, Bottom of Funnel, is the Decision-Making Stage. Hot prospects need clear offers, risk removal, and urgency to convert, powered by GHL workflows from Module 7. Drop-off between stages is normal and healthy. The funnel filters wrong fit prospects naturally, and your job is to optimize each stage independently to compound total conversions. And every automation you build in GoHighLevel from Module 5 onward serves a specific TOFU, MOFU, or BOFU purposes. Knowing the stage before you build determines everything from your message to your tool of choice. Coming up next is Lesson 5.2, Types of Funnels, Lead Generation, Sales, Webinar, Nurture, and Application. Now that you understand the three funnel stages, the next lesson shows you the five specific funnel types businesses use in the real world. And when to recommend each one. You will learn what separates a lead generation funnel from a sales funnel. What a webinar funnel looks like and how to match the right funnel type to any client situation you encounter. Choosing the wrong funnel type for a client wastes weeks of build time and misses the conversion goal entirely. Lesson 5.2 ensures you always choose the right one. Let's continue to Lesson 5.2.

The image suggests that the man is giving a presentation or lecture about music or a related topic.

The image presents a slide from a presentation on marketing automation, titled "Why the Funnel is Your Foundation." The title is displayed in bold black text at the top left of the image, with a subtitle below it that reads, "Every automation you build in GoHighLevel serves a specific funnel stage."

**Main Content:**

* A large inverted funnel graphic dominates the left side of the image, divided into three sections:
	+ **TOFU (Top of Funnel)**: Blue section at the top, labeled "AWARENESS"
	+ **MOFU (Middle of Funnel)**: Purple section in the middle, labeled "CONSIDERATION"
	+ **BOFU (Bottom of Funnel)**: Green section at the bottom, labeled "CONVERSION"
* To the right of the funnel graphic, four white boxes are arranged vertically, each containing a bullet point and a brief description:
	+ **TOFU Automations**: Lead capture forms, landing pages, welcome sequences
	+ **MOFU Automations**: Email nurture sequences, case study delivery, booking funnels
	+ **BOFU Automations**: Offer pages, urgency workflows, purchase confirmation

**Footer:**

* A dark blue bar runs along the bottom of the image, featuring white text that reads: "Without a funnel framework, you are building automations with no strategic direction"
* A note below this text states: "With a framework, every automation has a clear purpose & position"

**Logo:**

* In the top-right corner, a logo features the word "LIBRE" in green text, accompanied by a small green leaf icon.

Overall, the image effectively illustrates the concept of a marketing funnel and its various stages, highlighting the importance of a strategic framework for building automations.

The image presents a slide from a presentation on marketing strategies, specifically focusing on the "TOFU: Top of Funnel" stage. The title, "TOFU: Top of Funnel," is prominently displayed at the top, accompanied by the subtitle "Awareness Stage - Strangers become Visitors." The slide is divided into three sections: "WHO IS HERE," "WHAT THEY NEED," and "WHAT YOU BUILD."

* **Title and Subtitle**
	+ Title: TOFU: Top of Funnel
	+ Subtitle: Awareness Stage - Strangers become Visitors
* **WHO IS HERE**
	+ People who have never heard of your business
	+ Has a specific problem but no solution yet
	+ Broadly browsing and exploring topics
* **WHAT THEY NEED**
	+ Awareness that potential solutions exist
	+ High-value educational content
	+ Free value with absolutely no sales pitch
* **WHAT YOU BUILD**
	+ Blog posts & SEO content strategies
	+ Lead magnet landing pages (Lesson 4.5)
	+ Welcome email sequences

The slide provides a clear overview of the TOFU stage, highlighting the characteristics of individuals at this stage, their needs, and the types of content and strategies required to effectively engage with them. A dark blue bar at the bottom of the slide emphasizes that at TOFU, the focus is on educating and attracting, rather than selling. Overall, the slide offers valuable insights for marketers looking to optimize their approach during the awareness stage of the customer journey.

The image presents a slide from a presentation on the topic of BOFU, which stands for Bottom of Funnel. The title "BOFU: Bottom of Funnel" is prominently displayed at the top, accompanied by the subtitle "Decision Stage - Leads become Customers." 

**Key Components:**

* **Who is Here:** 
	+ Warm, engaged, trusts the brand
	+ Ready to make a final decision
	+ Needs that final push to act
* **What They Need:** 
	+ Clear offer with simple pricing
	+ Risk removal - guarantee or trial
	+ Urgency or reason to act now
* **What You Build:** 
	+ Sales pages and checkout flows
	+ Abandoned cart recovery workflows
	+ Booking automation sequences

**Key Takeaway:** 
At BOFU, you are removing FRICTION - they want to buy, make it easy and urgent.

This concise and detailed breakdown provides a clear understanding of the BOFU stage, highlighting the characteristics of leads, their needs, and the strategies required to facilitate their conversion into customers.

The image presents a comprehensive overview of the full marketing funnel, divided into three stages: **TOFU (Top of Funnel) - Awareness**, **MOFU (Middle of Funnel) - Consideration**, and **BOFU (Bottom of Funnel) - Decision**. 

**Comparison Factors:**

*   **Visitor Type:** 
    *   TOFU: Cold Strangers
    *   MOFU: Warm Leads
    *   BOFU: Hot Prospects
*   **Their Question:** 
    *   TOFU: "What is this problem and are there solutions?"
    *   MOFU: "Can I trust this company to solve it?"
    *   BOFU: "Should I buy this solution right now?"
*   **Your Goal:** 
    *   TOFU: Attract & Captivate → Get attention + email
    *   MOFU: Nurture & Educate
    *   BOFU: Convert & Close → Remove risk + friction
*   **Content Type:** 
    *   TOFU: Blog posts, Social Media, Lead Magnets, SEO
    *   MOFU: Email Sequences, Case Studies, Webinars
    *   BOFU: Sales Pages, Limited Offers, Booking Calendars

**Tool Section:**

*   A dark blue box with a white logo and the text "ON TOOL" is located at the bottom left corner of the image. 
*   Three modules are listed below the tool section: 
    *   **Form Builder & Forms** - Module 5
    *   **Email Campaigns** - Module 6
    *   **Pipelines & Workflows** - Module 7

The image provides a clear and concise visual representation of the marketing funnel, highlighting key factors such as visitor type, their questions, goals, and content types at each stage. The tool section suggests that the image is part of a larger presentation or course on marketing automation, likely using the Libre platform.

The image presents a comprehensive overview of the sales funnel, highlighting its role as a filter rather than a failure. The title, "The Funnel is a Filter - Not a Failure," sets the tone for the infographic, which aims to educate viewers on understanding why drop-off is normal and where their energy should be focused.

* **Sales Funnel Diagram**
	+ A pyramid-shaped diagram illustrates the sales funnel process, divided into three stages:
		- **SEE CONTENT (TOPS)**: 1,000 visitors
		- **OPT-IN (considering)**: 100 visitors
		- **PURCHASE INTENT**: 10 visitors
	+ A green text box notes that 5% to be left is common, and 2% to 3% is excellent.
* **Why This Is Okay**
	+ A list explains why the funnel's filtering process is acceptable:
		- The funnel filters out wrong-fit prospects naturally, saving sales team time.
		- Only people who resonate with your message continue to the next step.
		- 10 qualified buyers are worth more than 1,000 unqualified visitors.
		- Your job is to optimize each stage - not expect 100% conversion.
* **The Optimization Principle**
	+ A black box at the bottom of the image outlines the optimization principle:
		- Improve each stage by even 10 percent and total conversions can double.

In summary, the image effectively communicates that the sales funnel is designed to filter out unqualified leads, and its purpose is not to convert every visitor into a customer. By understanding this process and focusing on optimizing each stage, businesses can improve their overall conversion rates.

The image presents a presentation slide titled "Your Funnel Framework for This Course" with a subtitle that reads, "Every module from here forward maps to a funnel stage." The slide features colorful blocks organized into three sections. 

The top section, labeled "TOFU AWARENESS," includes "Module 5: Campaigns" and "Module 4: Landing Pages." The middle section, labeled "MOFU CONSIDERATION," comprises "Module 5: Funnel Design" and "Module 6: Email/SMS." The bottom section, labeled "BOFU DECISION," consists of "Module 7: Automations" and "Module 8: Analytics." 

The background of the slide is white. A logo for "LIBRE" appears in the top-right corner. A black banner at the bottom of the slide contains a green house icon and text that reads, "Coming Up in Lesson 3.5, you will design a complete funnel MOFU mapping all three stages. In Lesson 5.2, you will have hands-on use of MOFU to visualize & professionalize."

The image presents a concise summary of key takeaways from a lesson, titled "Key Takeaways" in bold black text at the top. The subtitle, "What you learned in this lesson," is written in smaller gray text below the title.

**Key Takeaways:**

* A marketing funnel is a model that maps the stages a person moves through from stranger to customer.
* TOFU (Awareness) is where strangers become aware of your brand. You go from educating and capturing their attention to creating traffic; discover tools using landing pages.
* MOFU (Consideration) builds trust and nurtures leads through further sequences, case studies, and thought leadership papers and webinars.
* BOFU (Decision) converts hot prospects by offering clarity, risk removal, and urgency via sales pages and incentives.
* Drop offs are normal and are a filter. If your lead's 100% convinced, they need to be equipped with the information to make each stage pass qualified meets their goals.
* Every qualification conversation serves a specific funnel stage purpose - identifying the stage dictates what you build.

**Skills Gained:**

* Understanding the 3-stage framework
* Identifying lead nurture bests
* Mapping automation to funnel stages
* Setting automation optimization

**Resources:**

* Funnel Framework Diagrams
* Automation Mapping Cheat Sheet

The background of the image features a white base with a green border on the left side and a green line at the bottom. In the top-right corner, a logo displays the word "LIBRE" in green text, accompanied by a small green leaf-like design above the letter "I."
